sanskrit_devanagari:
न मे पार्थास्ति कर्तव्यं त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन ।
नानवाप्तमवाप्तव्यं वर्त एव च कर्मणि ॥ २२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na me pārthāsti kartavyaṁ
triṣu lokeṣu kiñcana |
nānavāptam avāptavyaṁ
varta eva ca karmaṇi || 22 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.022 = MBh_06,025.022). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: O son of Pṛthā, there is no work prescribed for Me within all the three planetary systems. Nor am I in want of anything, nor have I a need to obtain anything – and yet I am engaged in prescribed duties.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Supreme Personality of Godhead is described in the Vedic literatures as follows:tam īśvarāṇāṁ paramaṁ maheśvaraṁtaṁ devatānāṁ paramaṁ ca daivatampatiṁ patīnāṁ paramaṁ parastādvidāma devaṁ bhuvaneśam īḍyamna tasya kāryaṁ karaṇaṁ ca vidyatena tat-samaś cābhyadhikaś ca dṛśyateparāsya śaktir vividhaiva śrūyatesvābhāvik

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.22 O Partha, na asti, there is no; kartavyam, duty; kincana, whatsoever; me, for Me (to fulfill); even trisu lokesu, in all the three worlds. Why? There is na anavaptam, nothing (that remains) unachieved; or avaptavyam, to be achieved.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: Krishna names himself — me — as the case beyond all dispute. There is no kartavyam (duty-to-be-performed) for him in any of the three worlds; nothing anavāptam (unattained) that he must attain. Yet — varta eva ca karmaṇi — he is engaged in action. The example serves the loka-saṅgraha argument: if the Lord, who has nothing to gain, acts, the realised one whose attention is on the Self has no warrant to refuse action.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.22 For Me, who is the Lord of all, who has all desires fulfilled, who is omniscient, whose will is always true, and who, at My own will, remains in the three worlds in the forms of gods, men and such other beings, there is nothing whatever to achieve.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: Krishna as the Lord, the supreme being, has no kartavyam in any of the three worlds, nothing to gain. Yet he engages in action freely — as the model for the people. This is the highest form of loka-saṅgraha: the Lord himself, beyond all need, acting precisely for the welfare of the world. The verse is therefore the strongest possible argument for the realised one's continued action.

disagreements_among_translators.word: varta eva ca karmaṇi (yet I am engaged in action)

positions.rendering: There is no work prescribed for Me within all the three planetary systems... yet I am engaged in prescribed duties
I have no duty in all the three worlds, nor anything to gain or attain. Yet I am engaged in action
Krishna as the Lord has no remaining karma; the example shows that even the one with no purpose-deficit acts, for the world's sake
Krishna as the Lord, beyond all need, freely engages in action as model for the world; this is loka-saṅgraha proper

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both traditions read the verse as Krishna offering himself as the limit case: the actor with no remaining purpose is still acting. Shankara reads 'me' as the Lord, the same Lord who is also the inner self of the realised one; the example therefore licenses the realised actor to continue acting. Ramanuja reads 'me' specifically as the Lord beyond all need, whose action is purely for the world's welfare. Translators preserve the surface but omit the doctrinal use the commentators make of the example.

literal_meaning: O Pārtha, there is no duty for me to perform in any of the three worlds; nothing unattained is to be attained by me; yet I am engaged in action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna offers himself as the proof-by-example. Even he — for whom no purpose is unmet, who has nothing to gain in any of the three worlds — engages in action. The verse establishes that action does not presuppose lack; the realised one acts not because something is unfulfilled but because action is the right answer to the actor's situation regardless of fulfillment.
